feed.title: Combined summary - BIP draft: CISA for Taproot Key Path Spends

entry.title: Combined summary - BIP draft: CISA for Taproot Key Path Spends
entry.summary: The discussed BIP draft proposes a transaction-wide cross-input signature aggregation (CISA) that introduces a new witness version to enable Taproot-style key path spending with options for signature aggregation. This approach allows each input to select between half-aggregation, full-aggregation, or to opt out explicitly through a marker byte in the witness. The specific schemes for these aggregations are detailed in BIP 458 and BIP 459. Standard script path spending remains aligned with BIP 341/342. The draft of this BIP can be reviewed [here](https://github.com/fjahr/bips/blob/cf0d4f2142cd0504b16e86739167b1f7ab9a3a06/bip-XXXX.mediawiki), and a mock pull request for inline comments is available [here](https://github.com/fjahr/bips/pull/6). Concerns were raised regarding the reservation of segwit version 2, as it conflicts with BIP-360 which also proposes using the same version. This overlap presents a potential challenge in harmonizing the two proposals, particularly in relation to post-quantum (PQ) cryptographic migration efforts. Questions have been asked about the possibility of generalizing the framework used for verifying aggregated signatures to support future PQ signatures that admit aggregation. Two potential pathways were suggested for implementing CISA in light of these concerns. One approach involves tightly coupling CISA with P2TRv2, which might incentivize users to migrate to wallets supporting post-quantum cryptography (PQC). This option appears simpler from an engineering standpoint but carries the inherent limitations of P2TRv2, including not being inherently quantum-safe and needing to address timing issues associated with quantum computing advancements. Alternatively, hiding the EC public keys behind a hash in the script-pubkey (SPK) was suggested, specifically using formats like P2MR or P2TRH, while attaching the EC public key in the witness of every aggregated transaction input. This method would allow the verifier to run the aggregated signature verification algorithms, offering increased security by relaxing the constraints associated with the quantum-day (Q-day) timing, albeit at a higher cost in terms of witness weight. Detailed calculations provided compare the witness weights under different aggregation scenarios, illustrating significant differences based on the chosen method.
